From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American lawyer

For the British politician, see [Sydney Silverman](/wiki/Sydney_Silverman "Sydney Silverman").

**Samuel Joshua Silverman** (25 September 1908 – 6 March 2001) was a three time [New York State Supreme Court](/wiki/New_York_State_Supreme_Court "New York State Supreme Court") Justice and career [litigator](/wiki/Litigator "Litigator") who was involved in several high-profile cases

## Background and early career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samuel_Silverman&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background and early career")]

Born in [Odesa](/wiki/Odesa "Odesa"), [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") (part of Russia at the time), his parents immigrated to [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") while he was still a toddler. Silverman graduated from with distinction from [Columbia College](/wiki/Columbia_College,_Columbia_University "Columbia College, Columbia University") in 1928, and earned his [law degree](/wiki/Law_degree "Law degree") from [Columbia Law School](/wiki/Columbia_Law_School "Columbia Law School") in 1930. Soon he became an assistant [corporation counsel](/wiki/Corporation_counsel "Corporation counsel") for [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")'s government. Shortly thereafter he became a partner at a firm that would become [Paul, Weiss, Rifkind, Wharton & Garrison](/wiki/Paul,_Weiss,_Rifkind,_Wharton_%26_Garrison "Paul, Weiss, Rifkind, Wharton & Garrison").

During his tenure Silverman represented Dr. [J. Robert Oppenheimer](/wiki/J._Robert_Oppenheimer "J. Robert Oppenheimer"), the [nuclear physicist](/wiki/Nuclear_physics "Nuclear physics"), at a [1954 loyalty hearing](/wiki/Oppenheimer_security_hearing "Oppenheimer security hearing") conducted by a panel of the [Atomic Energy Commission](/wiki/United_States_Atomic_Energy_Commission "United States Atomic Energy Commission"). Later, Silverman represented [Otto Frank](/wiki/Otto_Frank "Otto Frank"), the father of [Anne Frank](/wiki/Anne_Frank "Anne Frank"), in a lawsuit over a proposed stage adaptation of the famous *[Diary of Anne Frank](/wiki/Diary_of_Anne_Frank "Diary of Anne Frank")*.

## Later career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samuel_Silverman&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Later career")]

A [Democrat](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), Silverman was elected to state supreme court in 1962. He served there for four years before in 1966 becoming involved in a highly-public campaign with then-[Senator](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") [Robert F. Kennedy](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy "Robert F. Kennedy"), egged on by [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_New_York "Liberal Party of New York") leader [Alex Rose](/wiki/Alex_Rose_(labor_leader) "Alex Rose (labor leader)"), to end [patronage](/wiki/Patronage "Patronage") corruption in New York's [Surrogate Court](/wiki/Surrogate_Court "Surrogate Court"). Specifically, members of the Democratic and [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") parties were cross endorsing each other's candidates for the court in order to promote individuals who would skim commission from inheritance cases and introduce some of these funds back into the political machine. ("Don't die in the city of New York, don't die--if you want to leave anything to your wife and children," Kennedy exhorted.) Silverman won in a landslide, but his efforts at reform were hindered. In a 1967 [constitutional convention](/wiki/Constitutional_convention_(political_meeting) "Constitutional convention (political meeting)") Silverman's core proposal to abolish the surrogate court and reassign its jurisdiction to a rotation group of state supreme court justices was defeated. Silverman retired in 1971 from the surrogate court and returned to the state supreme court, frustrated by boredom, and reform failures, particularly frustrated with his hard-line senior colleague [S. Samuel DiFalco](/wiki/S._Samuel_DiFalco "S. Samuel DiFalco") (who was indicted for corruption charges in 1978, but died before the trial). Silverman was promoted to the Appellate Division in 1976. He remained at the state supreme court until he retired in 1984, when he returned as senior counsel for Paul, Weiss, Rifkind, Wharton & Garrison.

Silverman died at aged 92 at the Weill Cornell Medical Center of [New York-Presbyterian Hospital](/wiki/New_York-Presbyterian_Hospital "New York-Presbyterian Hospital").
